section: Office of Environmental Health and Engineering (OEH&E)
text:
The OEH&E was created in order to facilitate housing services in an expeditious manner in conjunction with our Self-Governance Compact. During FY 1994, the Absentee Shawnee Tribe submitted to Indian Health Services Addendum No. 3 to the CY 94, Annual Funding Agreement, and now receives funding for the construction of sanitation facilities. The principal role of OEH&E is to raise the health status of our tribal members to the highest possible level by providing sanitation facilities. Contact OEH&E at 405.214.4235 for additional details and information on the following services:

The Mission of the OEH&E Environmental Programs is to protect and preserve the environmental resources of the Absentee Shawnee Tribe of Indians of Oklahoma, both on tribal lands and within shared communities. OEH&E's primary focus is preserving cultural integrity and existing tribal sovereignty by building environmental capacity through tribal environmental code development and implementation.
section_path: Office of Environmental Health and Engineering (OEH&E)

text:
The OEH&E Office provides the following Water and Wastewater Service:

New Water Wells
Water Well Renovations
Community Water Line Service connections along with hookup and fees
New Septic Systems
Renovation of Septic Systems
Community sewer service lines with hookup and fees
Septic Tank and drain fields
Individual sewage lagoons
If you are having water and/or wastewater issues please fill out the following: OEH&E Office Application. If you have any questions please call our office at:  405.214.4235.

Tribal Brownfields Response Program
Water Pollution Control Program
Nonpoint Source Pollution Management Program
General Assistance Program (GAP)

The Brownfields Program is funded through the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency with a mission of empowering local communities, and other stakeholders in economic redevelopment by working together to prevent, assess, clean up, and sustainably reuse brownfield sites.

A brownfield is a property, the expansion, redevelopment, or reuse of which may be complicated by the presence or potential presence of a hazardous substance, pollutant, or contaminant. The Brownfields Program provides technical assistance for brownfields activities by protecting the environment while addressing potential sites to ensure the health and well-being of our Tribal members, the local community and environment.

You can find the public record of the current and past Brownfield site our program is currently working on here: Brownfield Public Record

Also, we have a facebook page that also contains brownfield information and sites we have completed and are currently working on. Click to like us at: Absentee Shawnee Tribe Brownfield Response Program Facebook Page

Clean Water Act § 106 Water Pollution Control Program authorizes EPA to provide financial assistance to AST to establish and administer programs for the prevention, reduction, and elimination of water pollution. AST collects water quality data on waters important to the Tribe. Data collected is analyzed for the health and safety of our environment and the AST community.

Clean Water Act § 319 Nonpoint Source (NPS) Pollution Management Program allows EPA to fund AST in order to mitigate or prevent polluted runoff. NPS pollution, unlike pollution from industrial and sewage treatment plants, comes from many diffuse sources. NPS pollution is caused by rainfall or snowmelt moving over and through the ground. As the runoff moves, it picks up and carries away natural and human-made pollutants, finally depositing them into lakes, rivers, wetlands, coastal waters and ground waters.

section: EPA Climate Pollution Reduction Grant Brian Bonanno, Sustainability Coordinator
text:
CPRG provides $5 billion in grants to states, local governments,
tribes, and territories to develop and implement plans for reducing
greenhouse gas emissions and other harmful air pollution.
Authorized under the Inflation Reduction Act, this two-phase
program provides $250 million for planning grants, and $4.6 billion
for competitive implementation grants.• Bernalillo County is partnering with the City of Albuquerque and several other local
partners to pursue CPRG on behalf of the metropolitan area.
• CABQ is the lead applicant and BernCo is a sub-recipient.
• (Phase I) CABQ was awarded an initial planning grant to curate partners and projects and
prepare an application for the Priority Climate Action Plan (PCAP) to be submitted spring
of 2024.
• PCAP prioritizes shovel ready or close to shovel ready projects, with the ability to scale
up as part of the CPRG Phase II
• BernCo has three projects included in the PCAP targeting EV infrastructure, energy
efficiency, pollution reduction, and stormwater management
Justice 40 : 
 To address the impacts of climate change more equitably and ensure
that funds from federal programs such as the Inflation Reduction Act
flow to historically disadvantaged communities, the Biden
Administration announced the Justice-40 initiative. This mandates
that at least 40% of the benefits of certain federal investments must
flow to disadvantaged communities.  
Justice 40 Methodology: Communities that bear an unequal burden or exceptional risk
in the following categories
• Climate change
• Energy
• Health
• Housing
• Legacy pollution
• Transportation
Project Proposal: Taking steps to reduce emissions, build greater resiliency, and
more equitably distribute sustainability resources and
infrastructure across Bernalillo County community centers.
Actions include:
• Energy efficiency improvements,
• Water conservation measures,
• EV charging stations
• Solar PV installations,
• Battery back-up systems.
section_path: EPA Climate Pollution Reduction Grant Brian Bonanno, Sustainability Coordinator

section: Energy Efficiency and Resilience Improvements for Community Centers Brian Bonanno, Sustainability Coordinator
text:
Where? • Prioritizing facilities
with highest EUI.
• Proximity to J-40
areas.
Mountain View Community Center:
• One level 2, dual port, EV charging station
• Battery back-up system sized to existing 72 kw solar installation
West Side Community Center:
• LED Lighting - 132 fixtures replaced
• Solar PV - 36 kW playground solar shade
• Building Envelope - 337 LF sealed penetrations /16 weather strip / 16 door sweep / 4
astragals
• Water Conservation - 20 fixes or improvements to valves, flow control, flush systems,
urinals
• Window Inserts - 18 window inserts installed
• Wi-Fi Thermostats - 9 Pelican Wireless style thermostats for equip like Split System, RT
• Gym Destrat Fans in gym
• One level 2, dual port, EV charging station
• Battery back-up system sized to solar installation
Los Padillas Community Center:
• LED Lighting - 105 fixtures replaced
• Solar PV - 31.5 kW carport solar array
• Building Envelope - 90 LF sealed penetrations/ 12 weather strips / 14 door
sweeps / 3 astragals
• Water Conservation - 22 fixes or improvements to valves, flow control, flush
systems, urinals
• Wi-Fi Thermostats - 12 Pelican Wireless style thermostats for equip like Split
System, RTU, or Furnace
• Gym Destrat Fans -1 HVLS fan installed in gym
• One level 2, dual port, EV charging station
• Battery back-up system sized to solar installation
Paradise Hills Community Center:
• LED Lighting - 131 fixtures replaced
• Solar PV - 39 kW roof mounted solar array
• Building Envelope - 230 LF sealed penetrations / 14 weather strips / 16 door
sweeps / 3 astragals
• Water Conservation - 27 fixes or improvements to valves, flow control, flush
systems, urinals
• High Efficiency Transformers - 150 kVA from 1 low voltage, low efficiency;
replaced to high efficiency
• Gym Destrat Fans - 1 HVLS fan installed in gym
• One level 2, dual port, EV charging station
• Battery back-up system sized to solar installation 
Los Vecinos Community Center:
• LED Lighting - 115 fixtures replaced
• Building Envelope - 76 LF sealed penetrations/ 5 weather strips / 5 door sweeps
• Water Conservation - 38 fixes or improvements to valves, flow control, flush
systems, urinals
• High Efficiency Transformers - 50 kVA from 1 low voltage, low efficiency; replaced
to high efficiency
• Wi-Fi Thermostats - 6 Pelican Wireless style thermostats for equip like Split
System, RTU, or Furnace
• One level 2, dual port, EV charging station
• Battery back-up system sized to solar installation 
Impacts:
• 394,814 lbs of CO2 reduction in the first year of implementation. Equivalent to
enough energy to power 22 homes for one year.
• Improved access to EV charging infrastructure for residents in nearby areas can
help to reduce some barriers to wider adoption.
• Solar installations coupled with emergency battery back-up will provide greater
resiliency in the face of increasing threats from climate change
• Improved operation of county facilities will reduce utility costs, conserve water,
improve overall comfort and extend longevity of the buildings and their systems.
section_path: Energy Efficiency and Resilience Improvements for Community Centers Brian Bonanno, Sustainability Coordinator

section: Public EV Fast Charge Stations Richard Meadows, Technical Planning Manager
text:
Proposed Locations
Route 66 Visitor Center: West Central & I-40
Near alternative fuel corridor; proposed retail in center
Alvarado Square Downtown: Silver Ave & 4th St
Near apartments, hotels, restaurants, retail, gov’t offices
Project Equity: Air quality related health issues have more severe impacts on low income and minority neighborhoods.
Particulate matter and ozone from ICE vehicles can cause asthma and heart/ cardiovascular disease.
Heat waves, drought, and wildfires associated with climate change increase air quality issues for residents.
Route 66 Visitor Center
• Tract 47.12 Southwest Mesa is economically and
equitably disadvantaged
• Population – 6,754
• Households – 2,232
• Poverty - 17.9% compared to 15.6% countywide
• Hispanic - 81% compared to 15.6% countywide
• Language - 57.3% speak language other than
English at home compared to 25.2% countywide
• Particulate matter 2.5 µg/m3 – 6.01
Alvarado Square Downtown
• Tract 21 Downtown is economically and
environmentally disadvantaged
• Population – 2,126
• Households – 1,298
• Disability – 28.2% compared to 14.3% countywide
• Poverty – 36.7% compared to 15.6% countywide
• Median Household Income – $17,107 compared to
$59,640 countywide
• Particulate matter 2.5 µg/m3 – 6.23
• Ozone ppb – 57.4
Project Need: Transportation Contributes 30% of GHGs
ICE Vehicles Emit 4.6 metric tons CO2 per year
Avg. household contributes 10 metric tons CO2 /yr 
NM New Car Market Share: 8% E.V.s
~11,000 E.V.s in Albuquerque & Santa Fe
Albuquerque has 153 level 2 and 47 level 3 (1/2 Tesla) chargers
All DC Fast Chargers in NE Heights (Level 2: 8 Downtown, 3 SW Mesa)
section_path: Public EV Fast Charge Stations Richard Meadows, Technical Planning Manager

text:

section: South Valley Street Trees and Green Stormwater Infrastructure Megan Marsee, Water Conservation Program Lead Kali Bronson, Stormwater Quality Program Lead
text:
Presentation Outline
1. Project description
2. Project precedents
3. GIS study to inform site selection
4. Input needed from South Valley residents
Proposed project • What: Street trees & green
stormwater infrastructure
• Where: Residential streets
impacted by flooding in
South Valley
• Why: Reduce localized
flooding, while yielding
many other co
-benefits
• How: Phased
implementation, beginning
with pilot project
Existing
conditions
• Rural-type
roads
• Wide right-ofway
• No sidewalks
• No curbs
• No gutter
• Limited onstreet parking
GSI Benefits:
• Reduces flooding
• Improves stormwater
quality
• Increases tree canopy
• Reduces urban heat
island effect
• Reduces greenhouse
gas emissions and air
pollution
• Creates wildlife
habitat
• Conserves potable
water
• Improves physical and
mental health
• Calms traffic
• Beautifies
neighborhoods
GIS study:
• County is currently conducting
GIS study to inform site
selection.
• Prioritization criteria include:
• Areas that experience flooding
• Existing tree canopy
• Sociodemographic,
environmental, and health
vulnerability indicators 
Input needed from South Valley residents:
• Does your neighborhood experience flooding during storm
events?
• Would your neighborhood benefit from installing trees and
green stormwater infrastructure along streets?
• Are you part of an organization that would like to partner
with the County on this project?
• What ideas, concerns, feedback do you have about this
project?
Find out more:
• StoryMap with project information
• Results of GIS study (coming soon)
• Project Feedback Form
• Contact information for staff
bernco.gov/SouthValleyGSI
